---
title: "Sector-level methane emission estimates using secondary tracer observations"
date: 2026-06-24
author:
  - name: "Alice Ramsden"
    affiliation: "Met Office"
  - name: "Alison Redington"
    affiliation: "Met Office"
  - name: "Alistair Manning"
    affiliation: "Met Office"
categories:
  - methane
  - inverse-modelling
  - isotopes
  - ethane
  - fossil-fuel
image: /research-highlights/figures/methane-sector-tracers-fig1.png
description: >
  GEMMA has tested whether adding methane isotope ratios and ethane observations
  to the InTEM inverse modelling system can separate fossil-fuel from non-fossil-fuel
  methane emissions. Whilst synthetic data tests showed the approach is theoretically
  viable, current knowledge of isotopic source signatures across the UK is not yet
  precise enough to improve on standard methane-only inversions.
---

::: {.pdf-download-bar}
**Download this page as a PDF policy brief:**
[Download PDF](methane-sector-tracers.pdf){.btn .btn-sm .btn-primary}
:::

::: {.highlight-meta}
**Authors:** Alice Ramsden, Alison Redington, and Alistair Manning (Met Office) \
**Published:** 24 June 2026 \
**Related publications:** [Using high-frequency observations of δ¹³C-CH₄ and δ²H-CH₄ and uncertain regional isotopic signatures to estimate sources of UK methane emissions](https://doi.org/10.5194/egusphere-2026-779); [Quantifying fossil fuel methane emissions using observations of atmospheric ethane and an uncertain emission ratio](https://doi.org/10.5194/acp-22-3911-2022)
:::

## Why this matters

Separating fossil-fuel (FF) methane emissions — from gas leaks, oil extraction,
and coal mines — from non-fossil-fuel (non-FF) sources such as agriculture and
waste is important for targeting mitigation policy effectively.

Conventional atmospheric inversion methods estimate total methane but cannot
independently distinguish between FF and non-FF sources. Secondary observations
carry information about the origin of the gas: methane isotope ratios (δ¹³C-CH₄)
differ between fossil and biological sources, and ethane is co-emitted with fossil
methane but not with biological methane. Incorporating these "tracers" into the
inversion system offers a route to sector attribution that is independent of
inventory assumptions.

This approach depends on the ongoing expansion of ethane and methane isotope
observations across the UK, and requires a well-characterised understanding of
source signatures — the isotopic composition and ethane:methane ratios associated
with each emission type.

## What GEMMA did

GEMMA tested whether adding methane isotope observations (δ¹³C-CH₄) to the
Inverse Technique for Emission Modelling (InTEM) system could improve estimates
of the FF and non-FF sector split across the UK.

**Synthetic data tests:** Using artificially generated observations based on
isotope data from Heathfield (HFD) and Invergowrie (IVG), the method successfully
estimated FF and non-FF methane emissions across the UK — an improvement over a
standard methane-only inversion. However, when realistic uncertainties were placed
on the source signatures, accuracy declined and results became more dependent on
the assumed prior sector split.

**Real-world tests:** Using real isotope observations and current knowledge of
source signatures, results showed no improvement over a standard methane-only
inversion. This indicates that existing understanding of source signatures across
the UK is not yet precise enough to constrain FF versus non-FF emissions.

![δ¹³C-CH₄ source signatures for fossil-fuel and non-fossil-fuel methane averaged onto the 25 km NAME model grid.](/research-highlights/figures/methane-sector-tracers-fig1.png){fig-alt="Two-panel map showing delta 13C-CH4 source signatures on the 25km NAME resolution grid: fossil-fuel sector on the left, non-fossil-fuel sector on the right, based on Royal Holloway and isoMET project observations."}

## Scientific and policy impact

This work establishes the conditions under which isotope-based sector attribution
is feasible and identifies the key limiting factor: the precision of source
signature knowledge. It provides a clear roadmap for the observational and
modelling work needed before this approach can contribute robustly to
policy-relevant UK emissions reporting.

As ethane and isotope monitoring expands through the GEMMA programme, the
conditions for making this method effective are progressively being put in place.

## What comes next

- Use ethane and methane isotope observations together in the inversion to provide
  greater combined constraint on fossil-fuel emissions.
- Use the InTEM system with ethane observations and fixed ethane:methane priors
  to test the approach in a system with reduced prior uncertainty.
- Continue incorporating additional ethane and methane isotope ratio observations
  from the UK network as they become available.
- Set more informative emission ratio and source signature priors to reduce
  overall uncertainty in the modelling system.
